critique.summary: The article claims that a 400 billion parameter large language model (LLM) is running on an iPhone 17 Pro, achieving 0.6 tokens per second. The demonstration is credited to several individuals on Twitter.
mainPoints: A 400 billion parameter LLM is running on an iPhone 17 Pro.
The model processes at a speed of 0.6 tokens per second.
The demonstration was shared on Twitter by user @anemll with credits to @danveloper, @alexintosh, and @danpacary.
critique.truthfulness: There is no publicly available evidence or credible technical documentation supporting the claim that a 400 billion parameter LLM can run natively on an iPhone 17 Pro with the stated performance. Current state-of-the-art large language models of this size require substantial computational resources typically only available on specialized hardware such as GPUs or TPUs in data centers. The claim appears to be a demonstration or proof of concept possibly involving model quantization, offloading, or other techniques, but without detailed technical explanation or verification from independent sources, the claim remains unverified and likely exaggerated.
considerations: The article does not explain how the model is run on the device (e.g., model compression, offloading computation to servers, or other optimization techniques).
No independent verification or technical details are provided to substantiate the performance metrics.
Running such a large model on a mobile device contradicts current known hardware limitations and energy constraints.
The source is a social media post rather than a peer-reviewed or technical publication.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the feasibility and implications of running a 400 billion parameter large language model (LLM) on an iPhone 17 Pro. Commenters debate the technical challenges such as heat, power consumption, and memory constraints, while also acknowledging the impressive nature of the demonstration and speculating on future hardware and software developments.

comments.author: russellbeattie
comments.text:
I have some macro opinions about Apple - not sure if I'm correct, but tell me what you think.

Apple has always seen RAM as an economic advantage for their platform: Make the development effort to ensure that the OS and apps work well with minimal memory and save billions every year in hardware costs. In 2026, iPhones still come with 8Gb of RAM, Pro/Max come with 12Gb.

The problem is that AI (ML/LLM training and inference) are areas where you can't get around the need for copious amounts of fast working memory. (Thus the critical shortage of RAM at the moment as AI data centers consume as many memory chips as possible.)

Unless there's something I don't know (which is more than possible) Apple can't code their way around this problem, nor create specialized SoCs with ML cores that obviate the need for lots and lots of RAM.

So, it's going to be interesting whether they accept this reality and we start seeing the iPhones in the future with 16Gb, 32Gb or more as standard in order to make AI performant. And if they give up on adding AI to the billions of iPhones with minimal RAM already out there.

As a side note, 8Gb of RAM hasn't been enough for a decade. It prevents basic tasks like keeping web tabs live in the background. My pet peeve is having just a few websites open, and having the page refresh when swapping between them because of aggressive memory management.

To me, Apple's obvious strength is pushing AI to the edge as much as possible. While other companies are investing in massive data centers which will have millions of chips that will be outdated within the next couple years, Apple will be able to incrementally improve their ML/AI features by running on the latest and greatest chips every year. Apple has a huge advantage in that they can design their chips with a mega high speed bus, which is just as important as the quantity of RAM.

But all that depends on Apple's willingness to accept that RAM isn't an area they can skimp on any more, and I'm not sure they will.

Sorry for the brain dump. I'd love to be educated on this in case I'm totally off base.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and plausible analysis of Apple's hardware strategy and its implications for AI workloads, highlighting the critical role of RAM and realistic challenges.

comments.author: johnwhitman
comments.text: The heat problem is going to be the real constraint here. I've been running smaller models locally for some internal tooling at work and even those make my MacBook sound like a jet engine after twenty minutes. A 400B model on a phone seems like a great way to turn your pocket into a hand warmer, even with MoE routing. The unified memory is clever but physics still applies.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly identifies thermal constraints as a major issue for running large models on mobile devices, supported by personal experience with smaller models.

comments.author: andix
comments.text: My iPad Air with M2 can run local LLMs rather well. But it gets ridiculously hot within seconds and starts throttling.
comments.analysis: This firsthand observation reinforces the thermal throttling problem on mobile devices running LLMs, supporting the broader technical concerns raised.

comments.author: yalogin
comments.text:
Apple’s unified memory architecture plays a huge part in this. This will trigger a large scale rearchitecture of mobile hardware across the board. I am sure they are already underway.

I understand this is for a demo but do we really need a 400B model in the mobile? A 10B model would do fine right? What do we miss with a pared down one?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid question about the necessity of extremely large models on mobile and highlights Apple's architectural innovation, though the optimal model size remains speculative.

comments.author: r4m18612
comments.text: Impressive. Running a 400B model on-device, even at low throughput, is pretty wild.
comments.analysis: Expresses amazement at the technical feat, which is factually accurate but lacks deeper technical insight.

comments.author: skiing_crawling
comments.text: I can't understand why this is a surprise to anyone. An iphone is still a computer, of course it can run any model that fits in storage albiet very slowly. The implementation is impressive I guess but I don't see how this is a novel capability. And for 0.6t/s, its not a cost efficient hardware for doing it. The iphone can also render pixar movies if you let it run long enough, mine bitcoin with a pathetic hashrate, and do weather simulations but not in time for the forecast to be relevant.
comments.analysis: Provides a realistic perspective that running large models on phones is possible but not novel or efficient, adding useful context.

comments.author: HardCodedBias
comments.text:
The power draw is going to be crazy (today).

Practical LLMs on mobile devices are at least a few years away.
comments.analysis: Correctly highlights power consumption as a limiting factor and the timeline for practical mobile LLMs, aligning with industry expectations.

comments.author: ashwinnair99
comments.text: A year ago this would have been considered impossible. The hardware is moving faster than anyone's software assumptions.
comments.analysis: Acknowledges rapid hardware progress enabling feats previously deemed impossible, a reasonable observation though somewhat general.

comments.author: firstbabylonian
comments.text:
> SSD streaming to GPU

Is this solution based on what Apple describes in their 2023 paper 'LLM in a flash' [1]?

1: https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.11514
comments.analysis: References relevant research which may underpin the demo, adding useful context though not providing direct evaluation.

comments.author: simopa
comments.text: It's crazy to see a 400B model running on an iPhone. But moving forward, as the information density and architectural efficiency of smaller models continue to increase, getting high-quality, real-time inference on mobile is going to become trivial.
comments.analysis: Optimistic but reasonable prediction about future improvements in model efficiency enabling mobile inference.

comments.author: causal
comments.text:
Run an incredible 400B parameters on a handheld device.

0.6 t/s, wait 30 seconds to see what these billions of calculations get us:

"That is a profound observation, and you are absolutely right ..."
comments.analysis: Highlights the slow throughput and limited immediate utility of the demo, emphasizing the gap between capability and practical performance.

comments.author: _air
comments.text: This is awesome! How far away are we from a model of this capability level running at 100 t/s? It's unclear to me if we'll see it from miniaturization first or from hardware gains
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm and curiosity about future performance improvements, though speculative without technical backing.

comments.author: redwood
comments.text: It will be funny if we go back to lugging around brick-size batteries with us everywhere!
comments.analysis: A humorous exaggeration about power demands, not a serious technical contribution.

comments.author: dv_dt
comments.text: CPU, memory, storage, time tradeoffs rediscovered by AI model developers.  There is something new here, add GPU to the trade space.
comments.analysis: Insightfully notes that AI development reintroduces classic system tradeoffs with the addition of GPU considerations.

comments.author: cj00
comments.text: It’s 400B but it’s mixture of experts so how many are active at any time?
comments.analysis: Raises a relevant technical question about the model architecture that affects resource usage and performance.

comments.author: rwaksmunski
comments.text: Apple might just win the AI race without even running in it. It's all about the distribution.
comments.analysis: Suggests Apple's advantage may lie in distribution rather than raw AI computation, a plausible but broad claim.

comments.text: "400 bytes should be enough for anybody"
comments.analysis: A humorous and irrelevant comment that adds no substantive value to the discussion.

comments.author: pier25
comments.analysis: Simply a link repost without commentary, providing no analysis or insight.
